profile.behavior: Hovers to feed, flies with rapid, precise movements, and catches small insects as well as taking nectar.
field_marks: Tiny, compact hummingbird with a long, slender, slightly downcurved dark bill.
Bright emerald to golden-green crown and upperparts.
Gray-white to whitish underparts, often with buffy flanks.
Adult male has an iridescent ruby-red throat that can look dark in poor light; female lacks it.
Short dark tail: forked in males; females show white tips on the outer tail feathers.
Large dark eye, very short legs, and long, narrow wings adapted for hovering.
profile.habitat: Open woodlands and forest edges, including meadows, old fields, orchards, gardens, hedgerows, and scrub.

sources.title: Ruby-throated Hummingbird Identification — All About Birds

findings: Species identity verified: the plate depicts an adult male Ruby-throated Hummingbird, Archilochus colubris (Trochilidae).

All visible text claims are supported: scientific and common names, family, length, weight, wingspan, green upperparts, gray-white underparts, red male gorget, male forked dark tail, female white-tipped outer tail feathers, habitat, hovering/nectar/insect behavior, and palette.

The illustrated male’s slender slightly downcurved dark bill, large dark eye, compact body, green crown/back, pale underparts, ruby throat, short legs/feet, folded long narrow wing, and forked dark tail are consistent with the verified species and the supplied references. The tail and feet are slightly stylized/over-emphasized, but not materially inaccurate.
Anatomy check passes: one head, one beak, two wings (one occluded in side view), two legs, and one tail; no extra or malformed structures.
No place name, ZIP code, coordinates, map, or local-observation detail appears on the plate.
